summary: Jeff Atwood explores the uncomfortable question of whether programmers should take jobs in the adult industry, drawing from a 1996 account of social ostracism faced by a porn site developer and his own near-miss with such a job. He acknowledges that adult sites often present genuinely interesting technical challenges—payment processing, scaling, continuous deployment—that exceed typical business applications. However, he notes that working in this space requires constant white lies about your employment. Atwood extends this discomfort to companies like IMVU that aren't technically adult content but trade heavily on sexualized marketing. He concludes by admitting his own difficulty respecting technical excellence when it serves 'least common denominator interests,' while acknowledging this may be his own problem rather than an objective judgment.
key_points: Adult industry programming jobs often involve more interesting technical challenges than typical corporate CRUD applications, including payment processing, scaling, and continuous deployment
Working in the adult industry creates social complications—colleagues, friends, family, and future employers may judge you negatively regardless of code quality
Programmers in this space learn to tell 'white lies' about their work, using vague descriptions and invoking NDAs to avoid uncomfortable conversations
Companies that aren't technically adult content but use heavily sexualized marketing (like IMVU) occupy an uncomfortable gray zone that can still affect professional perception
The author admits struggling to respect technical excellence when it serves purposes he considers lowbrow, while acknowledging this is a subjective and potentially unfair position
money_quotes: Does working on the adult part of the net mean I'm a scumbag? Does it mean I'm sleazy? Does it mean I'm untrustworthy? Does it mean my code is bad?
I think the operative words were 'what will we tell our parents?' It's a fair question.
When I asked him what the environment was like, working daily with adult products, he had a one word response: weird.

I don't think I'd want to work for a company that couldn't see the benefit of having someone onboard that's worked with systems that must always work, have a known cost-per-minute of downtime, and are on a permanently continuous release cycle.
If you choose to work in the adult industry, you have to tell white lies about your work—small evasions about what your work is, and who it is for, depending on the audience.
I can't help questioning the ultimate value of that work.
It's difficult for me to respect software engineering in the service of such least common denominator interests.
key_insight: The technical merit of your work exists independently from its social acceptability, but programmers must decide whether interesting engineering challenges are worth the personal and professional complications of working in morally ambiguous industries.
